chunks.title: Catalog Description / Special Course Designation / Additional Course Information / Course Copyright
chunks.content:
Software Engineering. (2-2). Credit 3. Application of engineering approach to computer software design and development; life cycle models, software requirements and specification; conceptual model design; detailed design; validation and verification; design quality assurance; software design/development environments and project management. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331 or approval of instructor.

None

Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and agreed to in the first 2 weeks of class.

The materials used within this course are copyrighted and include, but are not limited to, the syllabi, quizzes, exams, homework, online handouts, course videos, audio, and visual recordings of classes, etc. Because these materials are copyrighted, you do not have the right to copy or distribute these materials, unless permission is expressly granted.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content: Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 315 or CSCE 331 or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Apply the engineering approach to the design and development of computer software systems.
• Explain major software life cycle models and their appropriate use in project contexts.
• Document software requirements and specifications in a clear, testable, and structured form that captures user needs.
• Design conceptual and detailed software models that align with requirements and specifications.
• Implement and test software modules to verify functional correctness against documented requirements and validate them against user needs and project goals.
• Evaluate software products and processes for quality assurance, including planning for risks and quality, and for conformance to relevant external and internal organizational standards.
• Use industry-standard software development and project management tools to plan, monitor, and deliver software projects.
• Function effectively as part of a software engineering team, demonstrating professional responsibility and clear communication with stakeholders.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Optional
Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach.
Authors: Roger Pressman
Publisher: McGraw-Hill
Publication Date: 2019
Edition: 9th

chunks.title: Grading Policy / Group Project Grade / Individual Contribution / Quizzes / Assignments / Extra Credit
chunks.content:
The course simulates a software development organization with performance expectations that operationalize the Course Learning Outcomes listed above. Each performance expectation below is aligned with one or more Course Learning Outcomes (LOs).
• Understand factors that contribute to high quality software, including processes, methods, tools, and risk mitigation, monitoring, and management across the software engineering lifecycle from conception through deployment and support.
Success is measured by the team being able to deliver the most important features to stakeholders by the end of the semester according to the agreed to timeline, and at least a satisfactory stakeholder or user feedback**
In support of this outcome, below are sub-outcomes:
• Evaluate stakeholders' requirements as a basis for the design, implementation, and validation of a computing-based solution, with success measured by reaching at least an average satisfactory stakeholder or user feedback**.
• Build a computing-based solution, evaluate (e.g., test, review), using an appropriate software architecture and deployment environment, with success measured by zero high severity (i.e., no workarounds) errors discovered by stakeholders.
• Apply the most effective communication techniques to function effectively as part of a team, with people you have not worked with before, who may have diverse backgrounds/personalities, with success measured by at least satisfactory peer feedback**.
• Answer questions (impromptu or not) about the software engineering lifecycle, software engineering processes, methods, and tools; and how they contribute to developing high quality software, as indicated by the ability to communicate a clear, succinct answer with specific supporting examples.
** The feedback scores are from pre-defined surveys administered by the instructor.
This class is a 4-credit hour class and meets officially for a little over 4 hours per week (250 minutes). For every one credit hour in which you enroll, you will spend 2-3 hours outside of class studying. Therefore, a reasonable course load (including project work) will consume on average 6-9 hours outside of class. Total commitment for this class is 10 to 13 hours per week averaged across the semester.
The final grade you will receive in the class will be based on points accumulated during the semester. Thus, both continued progress on the process and the quality of your results will decide your grade.
The grading scale is A ≥ 90% > B > 80% > C ≥ 70% > D ≥ 60% > F.
We make every effort to provide you with the opportunity to earn an A (including opportunities for extra credit), so at the end of the course, an 89.9 is a B. We do not curve in this class. Rather, we teach proficiency which means that we have clear expectations in terms of what you should complete and what knowledge you should have obtained by the end of the course to succeed and to earn an A. If you are contributing appropriately and improving based on our feedback, you should expect an A.

Breakdown of the total percentage points in the class
| Category | Percentage |
|---|---|
| • Professionalism & Continuous Improvement - Engagement, communication, reliability, and use of feedback to improve your work and teamwork over the semester. | 25% |
| • Project Sprint 0 -15% Sprint 1 - 3% Sprint 2 - 7% Sprint 3-10% Project Turnover – 15% | 50% |
| ○ Governance - Understanding and applying project management, process, and quality practices to plan, monitor, and control the team's software engineering work. | 25% |
| Extra Credit**: See separate section below. | Depending on the complexity or time involved, 1-5 % point(s) is added to the final grade. For example, producing an educational video for the benefit of other students can be worth 1% points. |

You will work in a project group to develop a software application for intended users or stakeholders, applying an engineering approach to design, development, validation, documentation, and an end-of-semester class demonstration of the solution. The focus is not only on the final product but also on the process, project management, and teamwork.
The course simulates a software development organization, with you playing the role of a member of a project team. You will develop an application using appropriate software architecture and deployment environments for your intended users or stakeholders. Each project group must use the Ruby on Rails framework and PostgreSQL (and/or alternative technology as approved by the instructor).
The instructor team will act as managers and mentors.
The class will be divided into project groups. To ensure inclusiveness and avoid students feeling excluded, you will be assigned to a project group based on your lab section and the results of a skill survey (to be given when class starts) to help distribute students with prior experience in particular technologies evenly across project groups.
Project rubrics, used to assess your class performance, and a project notebook to guide you in the software engineering process will be distributed once the project groups are formed. Additional grading criteria may be added to the initial rubric; these will count as extra credit, and you and/or your team are under no obligation to fulfill the additional criteria. You can still earn a grade of A by fulfilling the basic requirements.
Because your teammates' grades are tightly coupled with your own commitment to the class, your grade is contingent on your equitable contribution to producing project deliverables as described in the project rubric. Expectations include:
• working system that meets stakeholders' expectations (as indicated by stakeholder or user feedback of at least "meets expectations") by the end of the semester.
• Final code that is clean and understandable submitted in the assigned GitHub Classroom repository at the end of the semester, along with any required supporting documentation (for example, readme, code comments, and similar materials).
• Submission of assigned documents (for example, requirements, designs, and project-management-related documents) to support understanding and proper execution of your system.
• Submission of a high-quality slide deck and recording of the final presentation, evaluated on the clarity of communication and demonstration of your system. These will be viewed by other students in the class and will be used as the basis for their end-of-semester reflection.
• Submission of reflections that are relevant and thorough (using the given template).
• Submission of other artifacts as required by the instructor (for example, project management related reports).
• Presentation of incremental progress during a project audit in each sprint conducted by the instructor team. These audits are mandatory and will be recorded. Students who are absent (see section “Graded Absence" below) will receive a 30% deduction from the group sprint grade unless the absence or lateness is considered excused.
• Submission of all required peer feedback and completion of all project deliverables on the dates specified in Canvas; these deadlines are intentional to ensure there is sufficient time to complete grading.
• Final course grades will not be released until all project deliverables are verified and accepted by the instructor team.
Your project grade can increase, based on (but not limited to) the following:
• Excellent project deliverables that exceed basic expectations.
• Excellent peer feedback and contribution to the group project.
• Effective, polite, and collaborative communication with the instructor/teaching team.
Your project grade can decrease, based on (but not limited to) the following:
• Poor stakeholder feedback or deployment of an application with high-severity defects (that is, with no acceptable workaround).
• The instructor team's perception of your effort. This can be due to poor peer feedback from your peers (after the validity of the feedback is confirmed), other measures of contribution, or other factors at the instructor team's discretion.
• Regularly missing your group meetings without notice.
• Failure to complete required peer feedback on time.
• Unprofessional or inappropriate (for example, rude) communication with instructors or classmates.

• This represents your individual proficiency during one individual project audit conducted towards the end of the semester, which will assess your knowledge on a specific topic(s) in software engineering.
• Each team member is expected to commit to the team's GitHub repository on a regular basis as specified in the project rubric.
• You are expected to thoroughly test / review your code and develop automated tests.
• Extra credit is given for those who assume the Product Owner and Scrum Master role, if the team grade is >=80%, and stakeholder/user feedback and peer feedback score are at least meets expectations.
• Each member of your project team will score and give feedback on the performance of the other team members through periodic peer feedback forms throughout the semester. Your peer feedback responses will be calculated along with the instructors' peer feedback scores and will be part of your individual project grade. NOTE: The instructor team has the right to withhold credit for late submission of peer feedback unless you have prior approval for late submission. You will receive two reminders. Failure to submit after the second reminder will result in a 5% deduction from your final grade, and cannot be reversed once the peer feedback grades have been posted in Canvas. This policy is in place to ensure timely distribution of valuable peer feedback.

Frequent quizzes will be given and will cover material from the homework, lectures, and any pre & post lecture work. These assessments let us know where your knowledge might be lacking so that we may be able to make class adjustments and inform you about the knowledge that you need to have. Once you have taken the quiz, you are not allowed to talk about its composition with anyone. Any unauthorized sharing is considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the honors council and result in the application of an appropriate sanction. In addition, quizzes must be done in class, otherwise, submission when not in attendance can constitute academic misconduct.

• Some assignments are expected to be completed individually, otherwise over collaboration may be considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the honors council and result in the application of an appropriate sanction.
• Assignments are usually due at midnight of the due date through Canvas, unless otherwise noted. Email submissions will not be accepted.
• It is your responsibility to make sure that the submission process is completed and that the correct assignment is submitted to the correct place. It is best to double check your submission by downloading it yourself, then confirming that the one stored in Canvas is the intended one. Failure to confirm correct submission is not a valid excuse for incomplete / late assignments
For late submissions, please refer to the section on Late Work Policy.

When needed to reinforce proficiency in concepts, extra credit assignments may be assigned, which will *all* count as extra credit. There is no obligation for you to do these assignments, rather these should be viewed as opportunities to boost your grade. Details on extra credit opportunities will be announced. Depending on the complexity or time involved, extra credit is worth additional percentage point(s) added to the final grade. To be eligible for extra credit points, you need to meet the eligibility criteria described below.
• Attendance must be 4 out of the 5 each from lab and lecture (i.e., in the Attendance category, your % points should be >= 4%, out of 5%).
• At least a satisfactory peer and stakeholder feedback rating
• Instructor discretion based on individual and team performance.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
Schedule subject to change. Check Canvas for the most up-to-date schedule and additional details. Labs will be conducted weekly unless otherwise announced.
| Week # | Main Themes | Major Deliverables / Milestones |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overview of the course; Introduction to software engineering & Agile; Intro to Rails | Skill Survey; Syllabus & Class Appreciative Agreement; Appearance Release |
| 2 | Project overview; Requirements & design; Testing & Quality; TDD introduced | Project groups announced; Team Working Agreement; Customer scope discussion |
| 3 | Risk analysis & stakeholder management; CI/CD; Quality techniques | Sprint 0 Scope, Backlog, UAT form, Risk Plan, and Data Design due |
| 4 | Correctness & Compliance; Ensuring integrity through authentication | Learning Outcomes Pre-Survey; Week 3 Lab; Book Collections due; Sprint 1 begins |
| 5 | Creating maintainable code; Usability & UX design; Efficiency; Preparing for Sprint 1 | Sprint 1 Project Notebook partial submission |
| 6 | Sprint 1 audits and reviews | Sprint 1 Deliverables & Deployment due; Sprint 1 Project Audit, Review, Retrospective; Sprint 2 planning and scope due |
| 7 | Project management - estimation, monitoring & control; Deployment & support planning | Peer Feedback 1; Sprint 2 Project Notebook partial submission |
| 8 | Sprint 2 audits and reviews; Sprint 3 begins | Sprint 2 Deliverables & Deployment due; Sprint 2 Project Audit, Review, Retrospective; Sprint 3 planning and scope due |
| 9 | Spring Break - No class | (Spring Break) |
| 10 | Preparing for User Acceptance Testing (UAT); Configuration management; Maintenance overview | Sprint 3 Scope, Backlog, Risk Plan, UAT form due; Mock UAT setup; Mid-semester evaluation |
| 11 | Project close overview; Project presentation timeline; Project turnover report | Sprint 3 Project Notebook partial submission; Peer Feedback 2; Quality of peer review evaluation |
| 12 | Project turnover; Final sprint ends; Customer UAT begins | Sprint 3 Deliverables due; Sprint 3 Project Audit, Review, Retrospective; Maintenance & Project Turnover phase begins |
| 13 | Project presentations; Course evaluation | Project Presentations (3 groups); Quality post-surveys (Compliance, Integrity, Correctness); CSE Capstone Expo |
| 14 | Project presentations (continued); Future of software engineering; Course evaluation | Project Presentations (continued, 3 groups); Quality post-surveys (Usability, Maintainability); Project Turnover Notebook partial submission; Customer UAT ends |
| 15 | Final project turnover deliverables; Maintenance phase ends; Official project close; Last lab day | Project Turnover Deliverables due; Reflection on presentations; Official Project Close; Extra Credit Educational Video due |

chunks.title: Graded Attendance / Late Work Policy / Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Since groups will be working on the project during lecture and/or lab, attendance is important, hence attendance is graded according to the following guidelines:
• Attendance will be measured through one or more of the following ways: electronically (e.g., Canvas Roll Call), hardcopy attendance sheets, quizzes, and submission of in-class exercises. At the end of the semester, 5 random days in the semester will be chosen as basis for attendance, for both lab and lecture.
• Absence and Tardiness: Failure to attend a class or late arrival (by definition > 5 minutes late) or leaving class early (> 5 minutes before class ends) without prior approval, may be counted as an absence and will impact your grade. Lateness or absence can be excused per student rule 7. Please inform the instructor at your earliest opportunity of your absence / tardiness in the event communication channels are down or you cannot inform in advance.
• Due to the unfortunate consequences and undue burden your frequent absences would have on the rest of the team, more than 5 unexcused absences from either lecture or lab are an automatic forfeit of the team grade.
• Excuses Not Valid: Oversleeping, cramming, etc., are not valid reasons. The instructor reserves the right to determine what constitutes a "valid reason" on a case-by-case basis.
• There will be no makeup for attendance unless the absence meets student rule 7.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

No "unapproved' late assignments will be accepted without prior approval and will receive a grade of 0. No late work will be accepted after the last day of class, irrespective of the deadline for an assignment.
With pre-approval from the instructor, late submissions are accepted only up to the time that the solutions will be discussed in class or as agreed to with the instructor depending on the specific circumstance. Late work approvals must clearly link to an excused absence request per Student Rule 7.
The request for deadline extensions should be submitted at least three business days before the due date, by sending a request via a link to be given to you by the instructor, especially for those absences that meet student rule 7. This will allow us time to review your request and ask for additional information when needed. Technology-related / technological problems nor failure to confirm correct submission are not an excuse for incomplete or late assignments.
*The only exception is in case of an emergency, where you cannot request in advance. In this case, you can submit details of your absence when you are able to.
Request for late submissions not related to absences can be considered. The instructor will base their approval on how well it meets the criteria described in Student Rule 7.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: Writing: Use of Al tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any Al-generated material that informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your reference list). Using an Al tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as academic dishonesty. See https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html fo r information how to properly cite Al tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts Amherst, https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-CTE GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf) Computer Code. In principle you may submit Al-generated code, or code that is based on or derived from Al-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments: you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. Al tools may help you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing. Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe (2023) “Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project http://sentientsyllabus.org..)
